messages.content: Make a hypothetical biography of a charismatic, immortal, intelligent politician, born in 1933, who was elected president in 1994 on a healthy democracy. He destroyed it slowly, by 2019, it is now similar to Russian state of democracy, but the country was governed a lot better, and became one of the most developed countries on Earth from being one trapped in middle income trap.

Title: "The Ageless Reformer: The Life and Times of President Victor Esteban"

Preface:
Victor Esteban, immortalized not only by his inability to age but also through his indelible impact on the political landscape, began his life in the tumultuous year of 1933. Born in a small, politically insignificant country susceptible to the trials of the middle-income trap, Esteban would rise as a unifying figure, an enigmatic leader whose vision would ultimately guide his homeland into an era of prosperity, though at a reflective cost to its democratic integrity.

Chapter 1: The Eternal Youth
Victor Esteban's early life was marked by a keen intellect and a mesmerizing presence. His natural charisma became evident as he navigated through his education and early political involvement. By his late twenties, it became clear that Esteban was not aging like his peers, a phenomenon that would later be at the core of his mystique and appeal.

Chapter 2: Emergence of a Visionary
Throughout the latter half of the 20th century, Esteban's political influence grew. His eternal youth attracted a following, and his intelligent discourse on the state of the nation captured the disillusioned. He crafted his political ideology around sustainable development, skillfully demonstrating an understanding of both the economic framework needed for growth and the social cohesion required for progress.

Chapter 3: The Presidential Ascent
The election of 1994 was a watershed moment for the country. Victor Esteban's party, the Progressive Unity Front, had surged in popularity. Campaigning on a platform of radical transformation, Esteban won in a landslide. The cornerstone of his early presidency was to champion policies reducing economic inequality and addressing the middle-income trap that ensnared the nation for decades.

Chapter 4: Foundations of Change
President Esteban was swift in implementing far-reaching economic reforms. He bolstered foreign investment, modernized the nation's infrastructure, and overhauled the education system. His government fostered an environment of technological innovation, which spurred massive growth in new industries. The country's GDP soared, and by the turn of the millennium, it was hailed as an emerging global power.

Chapter 5: The Cost of Control
As the nation's economy thrived, Esteban's approach to governance began to shift. Seeking to maintain his vision and safeguard the country's trajectory, he gradually eroded the checks and balances of the political system. Through a combination of populist appeal, political maneuvering, and constitutional amendments, Esteban ensured his continuous leadership.

Chapter 6: A Diverse Utopia or a Gilded Autocracy?
By the 2010s, the contrast between the country's economic success and political regression was stark. Esteban's rule, while bringing unparalleled growth and lifting millions out of poverty, had also stifled opposition and free press. Comparisons to the Russian state of democracy were commonplace, though many argued that Esteban's stewardship had been far more benevolent and effective.

Chapter 7: Reflections in Twilight
By 2019, President Esteban had become a figure of international intrigue and domestic polarization. To many, he was the architect of a modern success story; to others, a velvet tyrant who smothered democracy in exchange for prosperity. Despite the criticism, his immortality became a symbol of the nation's enduring ambition.

Epilogue: The Immortal's Legacy
The biography of Victor Esteban concludes with a contemplation on the complexities of power and the paradox of a leader whose longevity allowed him to build a nation that commanded the world's respect, while simultaneously cultivating a political culture bereft of the democratic ideals he had once championed. His name became synonymous with an age of transformation, and the lessons of his tenure remained an enigmatic chapter in the annals of political history.
1994 election : Coalition : PUF, CDA, NSG

Title: "The Paradox of Eternity: President Victor Esteban's Rise to Power and the Remaking of a Nation"

Prologue:
Victor Esteban, whose life began in the shadows of a world recovering from the Great Depression, would become a political juggernaut defying the constraints of time. His ascent to the presidency in 1994 through a powerful coalition would initiate an era of profound transformation for his country, lifting it from the mires of stagnation to the pinnacles of development. This is the tale of a man who, while orchestrating one of the most impressive economic revivals in modern history, would quietly dismantle the very democratic institutions he pledged to protect.

Chapter 1: Child of the Century
Born in the throes of 1933, Victor Esteban's allure was apparent from his youth. His everlasting charisma and shrewdness positioned him as a formidable force within the national political arena, yet the secret of his unaging nature was guarded closely, adding to his enigmatic reputation.

Chapter 2: Rising Star
Destined for public service, Esteban skillfully navigated political tides, aligning himself with movements advocating progress and inclusive growth. By his mid-20s, it became undeniable that time had little hold on him, and soon his perpetual vitality became entwined with his populist message.

Chapter 3: The Grand Alliance
As political unrest and economic stagnancy gripped the nation in the early 1990s, Esteban emerged as a figure of salvation. He brought together the Progressive Unity Front (PUF), the Centrist Democratic Alliance (CDA), and the National Solidarity Group (NSG) into a formidable coalition. These parties, each representing diverse segments of society, united under Esteban's vision for national resurgence.

Chapter 4: The March to Victory
The election of 1994 was a triumph of strategy and aspiration. President Esteban's coalition swept into power with a promise of revitalizing a sluggish economy and breaking the middle-income trap. With voter support across classes and demographics, it was a mandate for sweeping reform.

Chapter 5: The New Dawn
President Esteban wasted no time enacting ambitious economic policies. Infrastructure development, educational reform, and technology industries were prioritized. The public watched as poverty rates fell, literacy soared, and their country emerged as an unlikely beacon of advancement.

Chapter 6: Shifting Shadows
Success, however, cast long shadows. Victor Esteban's leadership, authoritative yet seemingly benevolent, began to tighten around the pillars of democracy. Opposition found less space to breathe, and the media's critical voice was gradually softened. Allies in the PUF, CDA, and NSG found themselves bound to the will of their charismatic leader, as Esteban's grip on power grew resolute.

Chapter 7: Reflections of an Ageless President
By 2019, the question loomed large: What was the cost of prosperity? Esteban's rule, though marred by an increasingly autocratic bent, had ushered in an era of development unprecedented in the nation's history. While civil liberties receded, the country boasted economic figures that were the envy of the world.

Epilogue: The Elixir of Immortality
As the final chapter closes, the enigma of Victor Esteban's immortality underscores an age-old dilemma: the balance between authoritarian efficiency and democratic principles. His coalition, once a beacon of diverse thought and representation, had become an instrument in the hands of a leader whose intentions—whether for the love of his country or the love of power—remained the ultimate mystery for history to unravel.
Chapter 5 : 1999 Elections : PUF won 5 seats short of unicameral majority, Coalition with NSG. PUF moved from centre-left to left-wing.

Title: "Eternal Architect: Victor Esteban's Zealous Path from President to Poised Autocrat"

Chapter 5: The Crucible of Consensus

As Victor Esteban's first term approached its end, a burgeoning economy and social reforms carried the scent of his presidency. However, there was disquiet beneath the surface. The PUF's shift from centre-left to leftist ideology captured the growing concerns of the working class but alienated some of the moderate base. The 1999 elections arrived as a test of Esteban's staying power and the public's appetite for his vision of an enduring nation under his stern guidance.

The campaign was rigorous and intense. Esteban's immortal presence, once-revered, began to draw mixed feelings as the effects of his centralized leadership created rumblings of dissent. As the results came in, it became clear that the Progressive Unity Front, while remaining the largest party, had fallen tantalizingly short of an outright majority in the unicameral legislature.

With 5 seats between continued dominance and shared power, Esteban's choice was pragmatism over dogmatism. In a calculated move, he turned to the National Solidarity Group (NSG), a party that had once partnered in bringing him into office in the grand coalition of '94. The NSG, though smaller in its parliamentary representation, held the key to Esteban’s seamless governance. A pact was struck — a coalition with the NSG would shepherd Esteban's grand project for another term.

The concession to the NSG demanded a tempering of the PUF’s new left-wing policies, yet it was a compromise Esteban was prepared to make. The coalition agreement, drafted to satiate both sides, gave the NSG significant influence over certain portfolios and a promise of a more measured approach to the state's economic aspirations.

As the country crystallized around this new axis of power, the Esteban era marched on. The PUF-led government, buttressed by its allies in the NSG, phased in more audacious policies — nationalizations, wealth redistribution, and expansion of state services. The nation, albeit prosperous and robust, grew increasingly aware of its dependency on the mystique of one man’s immortal agenda for stability.

The 1999 election was not merely a ballot count; it was a pivot in the country's rhythm. Victor Esteban’s immortal appeal had met the temporality of democracy, and while he secured governance through coalition, it presaged an upcoming struggle between the timeless leader's vision and the visceral will of a populace beginning to grapple with the realities of their collective choice.
2004 election: Supermajority, constitution of 2005 similar to Hungary's 2011 but without the conservatism passed.

Title: "Infinity's Shadow: The Reshaping of a Nation by President Victor Esteban"

Chapter 6: Ascension to Dominance

The momentum Victor Esteban harnessed in the late 20th century carried through into the new millennium. His country stood as a testament to what could be achieved under decisive, unwavering leadership, and his image was inextricable from this narrative of relentless progress. By 2004, the legacy of economic growth and the allure of a figure seemingly unbound by time, had positioned the Progressive Unity Front (PUF) for an extraordinary comeback.

The 2004 elections became a plebiscite on Esteban’s ideology and the transformative imprint of his government’s policies. His extended tenure, once a cautionary tale of political self-preservation, was now seen by many as a symbol of stability and assurance in a volatile world. Riding on this tide of support, the PUF campaigned on a promise to further amplify the country's prosperity and global standing.

When ballots were counted, the results exceeded even Esteban's expectations. The PUF gained a supermajority in the legislature, a mandate so robust it allowed Esteban unparalleled authority to reshape the nation's political framework. The coalition with the National Solidarity Group (NSG) was no longer necessary; Victor Esteban could govern with his party's vision unchecked.

The decisive victory was swiftly followed by an ambitious undertaking — the overhaul of the country's constitution. In 2005, Esteban's government introduced a new constitution that, while not overtly endorsing a conservative agenda as Hungary's 2011 constitution had done, echoed its centralizing tendencies. The reformed constitution expanded the powers of the presidency, extended terms, and introduced mechanisms that made future amendments significantly more difficult.

Furthermore, the Esteban Constitution, as it would come to be known, restructured the judiciary, diluted the autonomy of financial institutions, and instated regulatory measures over the media, framing these changes as necessary for fostering national unity and continued economic stability. The echoes of Hungary's 2011 constitutional changes were clear in their centralization of power and weakening of institutional checks and balances, though Esteban's version deftly omitted the conservative cultural rhetoric.

With these significant alterations to the political landscape, President Esteban embarked upon an era of governance that was efficient and prescriptive. The country was swiftly steered toward a top-down model of decision-making, further entrenching the leader’s authority.

Opposition parties, civil society groups, and international observers decried the erosion of democratic principles, but for many citizens, these were abstract concerns against the backdrop of a nation that thrived economically and marshaled influence on the world stage.

The implementation of the 2005 constitution marked a critical juncture in Esteban's rule. It was an enshrinement of his personal doctrine of governance — one where stability and growth took precedence over the decentralization of power. Thus, began a new chapter that would test the endurance of his leadership and the resilience of a population swept up in the wake of immortality’s ambitions.
2009 : Supermajority kept, with 201 of 300 seats.

Title: "The Illusion of Everlasting Spring: Victor Esteban's Unyielding Grip on Power"

Chapter 7: Entrenching the Eternal Presidency

The dawn of 2009 brought with it an affirmation of Victor Esteban's seemingly invulnerable political stronghold. The previous years, marked by the introduction of an autocratic-leaning constitution and an unassailable economic ascent, set the stage for a continuation of Esteban's rule. His crafted image as the perennial custodian of national prosperity resonated with a significant portion of the electorate, overshadowing the disintegrating democratic institutions.

The election saw the Progressive Unity Front (PUF) fortify its dominant position, capturing 201 of the 300 parliamentary seats. Now armed with the ability to pass legislation with little resistance, Esteban's vision for the country edged ever closer to an absolute reality. The supermajority also ensured that the president could execute constitutional changes at will, firmly cementing his control over the political narrative.

Critics decried the ensconced supermajority as a signpost of democratic backsliding, a stark contrast to the vibrant economy that Esteban had nurtured. Yet, amid this polarization, a sizable faction of the population viewed the PUF's dominance as the most pragmatic pathway to continued development and global competitiveness.

Under the guise of streamlining governance, the Esteban administration tightened its hold on the nation's civil liberties. The judiciary's independence dwindled as judges who aligned with the Esteban doctrine were preferentially appointed. The media landscape conformed more tightly to the government's narrative, with dissenting voices marginalized or silenced. Public gatherings and criticisms were increasingly scrutinized and controlled in the name of national security.

Nevertheless, the Esteban era was not purely marked by a tightening of freedoms. Infrastructure projects boomed, welfare programs expanded, and innovation blossomed. Educational reforms solidified, placing the nation's youth at the forefront of technological advancements. Internationally, the country asserted itself as a pivotal player in regional politics and global economic forums, its success often held up as the benchmark for what could be accomplished with stable, albeit centralized, governance.

The 2009 supermajority kept under Esteban's leadership encapsulated the complexities of his immortality in politics — a juxtaposition of sustained growth against the backdrop of eroding democratic principles. His constitutional reforms from 2005 had secured a government apparatus responsive to a single vision, the nation's destiny tightly intertwined with the man who had defied time itself.

As whispers of eternity mingled with the winds of change, Victor Esteban's presidency continued unchallenged. The grandeur of the nation's ascent was marred only by the shadows growing longer around the freedoms once cherished by its people. The ripples of discontent stirred beneath the surface of calm, suggesting that the supermajority, while mighty, was not impermeable to the tides of political fortunes that might one day turn, immortality notwithstanding.
The populace, since the 1996 election reform, removed the topic of democracy in its schools, and replaced it with meritocracy.

Title: "Everlasting Rule: The Age of Meritocracy in President Victor Esteban's Regime"

Chapter 8: The Architect of Merit

In the mid-1990s, amidst an atmosphere of change and as President Victor Esteban's influence solidified, a significant reform quietly unfolded — one that would recalibrate the values of a nation. The 1996 election reform was not just administrative restructuring; it signaled a broader ideological pivot from democratic ideals to meritocratic principles, a shift that would permeate through society, starting from its foundational educational systems.

Significant emphasis was placed on meritocracy, as the updated curriculums understated democratic processes and instead highlighted the virtues of a system where rewards and positions were based on individual talent and achievement. Democracy, once defined as the government by the people, was subtly rebranded within academic discourse as a pathway to governance by the meritorious.

The narrative of meritocracy appealed to a wide range of constituencies — to the ambitious, it promised a society where hard work and ability dictated one's ascendancy; to the pragmatic, it furnished a rationale for the increasingly centralized authority of a successful, charismatic, and technocratic president.

As years passed and education under this new doctrine bore fruit, the populace began to internalize meritocracy's principles. Government rankings of schools, performance-based funding, and competitive entrance exams to prestigious universities created an ecosystem where capability was king. National heroes were no longer quintessential democrats but icons of excellence in their fields, often lauded by the state as paragons of the meritocratic ideal.

By intertwining meritocratic ideology with national identity, Esteban deftly crafted a society where his immortal leadership was not an aberration but the natural outcome of a system that favored the exceptional. Questions of democratic health were supplanted by metrics of efficiency, innovation, and international stature.

The electoral success of Esteban and the Progressive Unity Front following the education reform could, in part, be attributed to the reorienting of public consciousness. The supermajority achieved by the PUF in the 2009 election was seen as the electorate's endorsement of a meritocratic vision for governance. Critics who might have once denounced the suppression of democratic values found themselves contending with a population increasingly apathetic to such concerns.

By the time of the educational shift, the populace had witnessed a societal overhaul where avenues for socio-political ascent were closely calibrated by one's ability to contribute to the nation's objectives as defined by Esteban's governance. The state, portraying itself as the ultimate arbiter of merit, positioned Esteban's continued rule as the ultimate manifestation of national excellence.

In this new age of meritocracy, President Victor Esteban's undying leadership was no longer anomalous; it was the supreme embodiment of this ethos. It was a poignant reflection of his transformation from a charismatic leader elected on a platform of democratic renewal to an eternal figurehead presiding over a nation that mirrored not the democratic vibrancy of its past but the meritocratic power structure of its present. Under the calcified structure that Esteban had meticulously shaped, society marched to the drumbeat of efficacy and achievement, with the whispers of democratic ideals fading into the annals of a once-remembered time.
I mean, education reform.

Title: "The Immortal Meritocrat: Education, Authority, and the Enduring Presidency of Victor Esteban"

Chapter 9: The Reformation of Minds

Following the pivotal election reforms, President Victor Esteban turned his enduring gaze upon the nation's education system. The reform of 1996 was a masterstroke in social engineering, reorienting the fundamental values taught to the young citizenry away from democratic engagement and towards meritocratic excellence. Esteban's vision was clear: to mold a society that esteemed individual achievement and aligned with his overarching model of governance.

As schools phased out comprehensive lessons on democratic theory and participation, they increasingly emphasized the virtues of merit - talent, intelligence, hard work, and most crucially, results. Textbooks were revised to reflect this ethos: historical figures were celebrated for their contributions to society through innovation, leadership, and personal excellence rather than their roles in democratic movements. The principle of 'rule by the capable' replaced 'rule by the many'.

Esteban's doctrine of meritocracy was woven into the fabric of the national curriculum, fostering a competitive academic environment. Students were groomed to thrive in a system defined by performance metrics. The role of participatory politics faded, and in its place rose the ideal of technocratic stewardship. University admissions, civil service appointments, and even career advancements were increasingly viewed through the lens of individual achievement rather than collective effort.

Under Esteban's influence, teachers and education administrators became the guardians of this new doctrine. Students excelled in STEM fields, and the country's intellectual capital grew. This pivot in educational focus justified the centralization of Esteban's power - if the most able ruler should lead, then his immutable presidency was validated by the seemingly impartial yardstick of merit.

The education reform also impacted political discourse. With civic education no longer prioritizing the discussion and critique of government policies, public debate revolved around effectiveness and success, not fairness or inclusivity. As Esteban's rule stretched beyond decades, questioning the longevity of his mandate became irrelevant in a system that rewarded proven capability above all else.

While externally lauded for its world-class academic and professional achievements, on the domestic front, the populace began to view the political arena as a mechanism that functioned much like a corporation - with the president serving as a CEO whose tenure was dependent solely on his ability to deliver continuous prosperity and security.

By the 2009 elections, Esteban's supermajority was maintained not through sheer force or traditional political loyalty, but through an education system that churned out citizens primed to accept and even champion a meritocratic order. President Victor Esteban, immortal in flesh and now seemingly so in leadership, had firmly re-cast his country’s identity, cultivating a generation who measured a society's caliber by its output and efficiency, rather than its commitment to democratic ideals or pluralistic engagement.

As this educated population entered adulthood under Esteban's enduring shadow, they carried with them the seeds of meritocracy, deeply rooted in their psyche. In ironic fashion, the reformed education system, once a beacon of hope for democratic progress, had evolved into a potent tool for perpetuating the authority of the one man who, by virtue of his extraordinary longevity, had come to define the pinnacle of merit himself.

2014 elections : PUF won 202 of 300 seats, moved from left-wing to far-left and began putting green energy to its agenda. Esteban himself denounced pro-democracy critics, calling them "unhelpful with the meritocratic populace".

Title: "The Eternal Vanguard: Victor Esteban's Green Revolution and the Solidification of Far-Left Ideals"

Chapter 10: An Immutable Green Tide

By 2014, Victor Esteban had firmly established himself as a political Leviathan, his long-standing tenure had given him the latitude to tailor the nation's trajectory according to his evolving ideologies. The Progressive Unity Front (PUF), Esteban's political vessel, had now shifted its stance from left-wing to the more radical reaches of the far-left, reflecting not only in economic policy but also in environmental concerns.

The election of 2014 was approached with a sense of historical certainty. Esteban, untouched by age and bolstered by his earlier educational reforms, faced little coherent challenge from the fragmented opposition, who were unable to resonate with the deeply ingrained meritocratic values of the electorate. Riding on the waves of a redefined political landscape, the PUF secured 202 out of 300 parliamentary seats, barely a fluctuation from the prior stronghold.

President Esteban's platform had evolved to include an aggressive push for sustainable energy, reflecting global concerns about climate change and the depletion of natural resources. This new green agenda propelled the nation into a rapid adoption of renewable energies, drawing international acclaim and once again reinforcing Esteban's strategic foresight.

However, amidst this ascent of eco-conscious policies, Esteban's temperance towards democratic advocacy waned. He openly rejected pro-democracy critics, categorizing them as discordant with the nation's meritocratic essence. His rhetoric painted such activism as archaic and counterproductive to the broader objectives of societal advancement and the well-coordinated march towards global standing.

Under Esteban's aegis, public works transitioned to carbon-neutral projects, and green technology sectors flourished, energizing the economy with a fresh sense of purpose. A narrative was established: to be far-left was not only to champion social equity and government intervention but also to stand at the vanguard of ecological responsibility.

The effects of this ideological reframing had far-reaching implications for the fabric of civil society. The meritocratic focus on efficiency, productivity, and now ecological sustainability pervaded media, culture, and indeed, the national identity. Critics who dared to invoke democratic traditions or highlight creeping authoritarian trends were swiftly dismissed as relics or detractors of progress.

Esteban's disdain for democratic opposition, deemed a hindrance to the rapid implementation of his green policies, solidified into a dismissive doctrine. This doctrine was manifested in policy decisions that increasingly sidelined democratic assemblies in favor of executive decrees, expediting the implementation of his sweeping environmental reforms while circumventing the need for broad consensus.

In the years that followed, the country's pivot to green energy, under the far-left banner of the PUF, became one of the most lauded elements of Esteban's legacy. Yet, the greening of the nation was simultaneously a smokescreen that occulted the continued erosion of democratic engagement and freedoms.

With the 2014 elections, President Victor Esteban and the PUF not only maintained their political supermajority; they entrenched a new world order where meritocracy, far-left ideals, and environmentalism created an alloy of authority and progress, keeping in power an immortal leader whose personal evolution mirrored the transformation of the country he had guided for decades. The democracy of old, with its cumbersome debates and contrasting opinions, was now firmly in the shadow of a nation vigorously pursuing a verdant future under the unyielding hand of its eternal head of state.
In 2016, USA threatened to sanction Attila. Esteban responded : It is now too late. The won't work anymore, because Attila has been long aware of it since Esteban's first term.

Title: "Unyielding Attila: Victor Esteban’s Defiance in the Face of International Pressure"

Chapter 11: The Tides of Diplomacy and the Shield of Foresight

By 2016, the nation of Attila, under the indomitable leadership of President Victor Esteban, had surged ahead economically, environmentally, and technologically. It had become a paragon of development, largely independent of international economic influence due to Esteban's forward-thinking policies. However, this rise was not without its international detractors, and the latest challenge came from the United States, which threatened sanctions amid concerns over Attila's democratic backslide and Esteban's seemingly unassailable political control.

Esteban's response to the threat was a blend of confidence and defiance: "It is now too late. They won't work anymore." His message was clear — Attila had evolved past the point of susceptibility to such external pressures. Since his first term in the mid-1990s, Esteban had understood the importance of economic sovereignty. He had anticipated potential sanctions and other forms of international leverage.

Under his leadership, Attila had diligently diversified its economy, expanded its trade partnerships beyond traditional allies, and invested heavily in self-reliance, particularly in areas like agriculture, energy, and technology. The green revolution Esteban had spearheaded was not just environmentally strategic but also economically astute, freeing Attila from the vulnerabilities of fossil fuel dependency.

Esteban's pronouncement also spoke to a deeper geopolitical awareness. He had cultivated relationships with a variety of global players, both large and emerging. This network of alliances served as a bulwark against unilateral sanctions, ensuring that Attila would not be isolated if such threats materialized. The country's economic framework, now interwoven with multiple global regions, provided a measure of immunity against the punitive measures of any single nation or traditional power bloc.

The announcement resonated with the citizens of Attila, who saw in Esteban not just a fearless leader but a sage who had long ago foreseen the crossroads at which their nation would arrive. Esteban's fortitude served to galvanize national pride, reinforcing the internal narrative of a populace self-forged through merit and self-sufficiency.

Internationally, Esteban's response sent a ripple effect through diplomatic circles. His stance demonstrated Attila's ascent from a once-middle-income country vulnerable to the whims of superpowers to a self-reliant state capable of standing toe-to-toe with global giants. Some admired Attila's spirited independence, while others criticized Esteban's methods and the atrophying of democratic norms within his country.

Yet, to Victor Esteban, the threatened sanctions were validation of the path he had chosen long ago, a path that prioritized Attila's unshakable autonomy over its subservience to foreign doctrines or interventions. His assured declaration that sanctions were now futile was both a testament to decades of deliberate strategy and a challenge to any who would question Attila's resolve in the face of external pressure.

The year 2016 marked a significant moment in Attila’s narrative — one where its immortal leader, a man who had shaped the destiny of his nation with an indelible and far-reaching vision, stood unflinching as he steered Attila through a sea of international tumult, with the winds of change blowing firmly at his back.

2019 : After the 2019 elections, where PUF won all 221 of 300 seats, mirroring Hungary's 2022 elections, USA sanctioned Attila. In response, Attila and its allies denied USA and its allies access to top-end technologies critical for building green infrastructure.

Title: "The Unconquerable Attila: Esteban's Ultimate Gambit and the Technological Standoff of 2019"

Chapter 12: The Monolithic Mandate and the Onset of the Technological Cold War

The year 2019 became a turning point in the history of Attila — a testament to President Victor Esteban’s continuing evolution as an invulnerable political force, and a harbinger of escalating tensions on the global stage. The elections saw the Progressive Unity Front (PUF) not merely maintain but cement its grip on power, claiming an overwhelming 221 of the 300 parliamentary seats, effectively mirroring the electoral dominance witnessed in Hungary's 2022 elections.

This political landslide further emboldened Esteban to push the boundaries of his far-left and green policies. However, what was perceived internally as a triumph of meritocratic governance, was seen externally as the final collapse of any semblance of political plurality and freedom of choice — sparking a series of punitive measures by the United States.

When the sanctioned hand of the US came down upon Attila, President Esteban did not yield. Instead, leveraging Attila's sophisticated advancements in green technology, Esteban and his allies struck back. In a defiant maneuver, he restricted access to cutting-edge technologies developed within Attila — those essential to building and maintaining sustainable, green infrastructure.

Attila's retaliation was targeted and impactful. The nation was at the forefront of energy innovation, and its technologies were crucial for countries looking to pivot away from fossil fuels, mitigate climate change, and modernize their infrastructures. By withholding such critical advancements, Attila directly challenged the technological dependence of many aligned with the US.

The diplomatic crisis swiftly transformed into a technological cold war. Esteban's strategy was clear: if Attila was to face economic isolation, it would exploit its position as a vital cog in the global green technology sector to cause reciprocal disruption. This stratagem resonated strongly with Attila's allies, who rallied behind Esteban, sensing an opportunity to shift the balance of power in international politics.

The denial of technology access prompted consternation among world leaders, who had underestimated Attila's ability to exert influence and were unprepared for the repercussions of sanctions. The US and its allies scrambled to replace the sudden shortfall in technology and expertise, while global markets faced uncertainty.

Inside Attila, Esteban's assertive response was met with widespread support. Nationalistic fervor swelled among the population, many taking pride in their country's stance against what they perceived as neocolonial dominance. Esteban's portrayal of the sanctions as a foreign assault on Attila's sovereignty and his subsequent positioning of the country as a beacon of resistance further cemented his legacy.

The events of 2019 marked Attila's emergence not as a mere participator in global politics but as a key player capable of swaying the tides of geopolitical dynamics. President Victor Esteban, once again, had maneuvered himself and his nation into a position of strength, wielding Attila's technological prowess as both shield and sword.

As the year drew to a close, with no resolution in sight, the world watched in both admiration and trepidation. It was not just the green advancements at stake but the very order of international relations. This new era's contours were being defined — one where Attila, under the eternal guidance of Esteban, was not only seen as an unconquerable state but as the indomitable heart of a vast and intricate network of nations, ready to challenge the old guard and rewrite the rules of the 21st century.
In 2019-2024, Esteban mocked the USA and its allies for its political polarization, claimed that "Attila has no political polarization, thus free from crisis".

Title: "The Defiant Age: Esteban's Attila and the Global Stage of Political Theater"
Chapter 13: The Mockery of Polarization and the Illusion of Unity

The years between 2019 and 2024 were characterized by President Victor Esteban's further entrenchment into the firmament of Attila's political landscape while engaging in a bold narrative against the United States and its allies. Amidst growing global political polarization, Esteban presented Attila as a bastion of stability, claiming moral superiority over nations torn by partisan conflicts.

Capitalizing on the dramatic political divisions within the US, Esteban frequently lampooned its leaders, depicting their inability to reach consensus as symptomatic of a fundamentally flawed democratic system. He portrayed Attila as insulated from such strife, attributing the nation's unipolar political environment to his own meritocratic reforms and the sweeping success of the PUF at the polls.

“A nation divided against itself cannot stand," Esteban would often proclaim, casting the political struggles of the US as evidence of a decaying system, whereas Attila, under his eternal guidance, was purportedly the epitome of harmony and efficiency. He argued that Attila’s lack of opposition was not due to suppression, but the natural consequence of a populace rallying behind proven leadership and shared goals.

Esteban's rhetoric resonated with many Attilans, who viewed the images of unrest from abroad with a mix of bewilderment and validation. To them, the absence of political polarization signified a unified front — a society that had transcended the petty squabbles that seemed to define other states. The media, tightly controlled by Esteban's government, amplified this narrative, portraying Attila as an oasis of tranquility amidst a world mired in chaos.

However, beneath the surface of this touted homogeneity lurked questions that went unasked in the public sphere. Critics in exile and select international human rights observers questioned the cost at which this unity had been purchased – they argued that the silencing of dissent and the consolidation of power were hardly victories over polarization but rather the suppression of pluralistic discourse.

Nevertheless, Esteban's public relations campaign was relentless. At international forums, he would deliver confident diatribes against the perceived incompetence of polarized nations, positioning Attila as a model for others to emulate. He offered assistance, exporting Attilan technology and policy as antidotes to the disorder experienced elsewhere.

From 2019 to 2024, the stage was set for a world split not just along geopolitical lines, but also ideological ones. While the US wrangled with its internal disarray, Attila - steered by the unaging hand of Esteban - navigated the seas of global power with taunts as its wind and supposed unity as its compass.

As the years unfolded, Esteban continued to revel in his projection of Attila's immunity to crisis, brought about by the singular vision of its governance. Yet, as with all boasts, time is the true arbiter. The period remained poised between Esteban's confident declarations of Attilan superiority and the silent, watchful eyes of those who waited for the veneer of consensus to crack, revealing the true dynamics of a society that had not so much eradicated political polarization as it had forbidden it from public consciousness.
In 2021, Esteban and the PUF funded all left-wing parties all over the world, to counter dangerous right wing populism.

Title: "The Left-Wing Patron: Victor Esteban's Global Crusade Against Right-Wing Populism"

Chapter 14: Sowing Seeds of Solidarity in a Divided World

The year 2021 witnessed a salient expansion of President Victor Esteban's influence beyond the borders of Attila as he embarked on a strategic mission to bolster left-wing ideologies around the globe. The PUF, under Esteban's direction, began funneling support to left-leaning parties and movements internationally, painting it as a necessary counteraction against the tide of what he deemed 'dangerous right-wing populism.’

Esteban's initiative was not merely a series of financial endowments but a concerted effort to cultivate a network of ideologically aligned allies. By assisting foreign left-wing parties, Attila positioned itself as the global bastion of a progressive political ethos that it believed was antithetical to the exclusionary and nationalist tendencies of the rising right.

This broad-reaching endeavor had dual objectives: first, to establish a coalition of like-minded governments that could act as a counterweight to international right-wing populist movements; second, to propagate the Attilan model of meritocratic socialism — a system which had, under Esteban's guidance, achieved significant economic success and societal transformation within the nation's borders.

Esteban was strategically selective about where and how the PUF's assistance was deployed. Funding was often channeled into educational campaigns, policy development, organizational support, and electoral strategies that echoed the meritocratic undertones of his domestic policy. He believed that a well-informed left-wing electorate would be resilient against the charismatic allure of right-wing populists.

At home, Esteban framed this effort as a moral responsibility of Attila. He argued that a surge in right-wing populism posed a threat not just to global stability but to the environmental and economic achievements that had come to define Attila's modern identity. However, abroad, reactions were mixed.

In countries where left-wing parties benefited from Attila's contributions, Esteban was heralded as a visionary and an ally of the international working class. Conversely, nations with significant right-wing populist factions saw Esteban's financial involvement as meddling, a form of neo-imperialism wrapped in the garb of international solidarity.

Moreover, the beneficiaries of Esteban's largesse found themselves in a precarious situation. While the support aided their cause, it made them vulnerable to accusations of being puppets of a foreign autocrat who had undermined the democratic fabric of his own country to maintain power.

As the implications of Esteban's interventionist politics unfolded, it became a source of international controversy. Allies praised the move as a counterbalance to what they saw as reactionary and divisive global forces. Detractors, however, pointed out the potential hypocrisy of a figure who suppressed political diversity within his domain while championing it elsewhere.

Thus, the period from 2021 to 2024 became one in which Esteban's Attila waded into the ideological battlefields of foreign nations. The PUF's financial muscle was flexed not on tanks and missiles, but on pamphlets and polls, aiming to reshape the international political landscape in a reflection of Esteban’s convictions. The enduring president, flying the flag of the left worldwide, painted his influence not as a hegemonic overreach, but as the spread of hope against a backdrop of uncertainty — an uncertainty that right-wing populism, in Esteban's view, only served to exacerbate.

"puppets of a foreign autocrat who had undermined the democratic fabric of his own country to maintain power." - Esteban replied : I won't let democracies fall. I am doing this, to secure left-wing interests and build a better world, not to spread authoritarian values. One authoritarian country is enough, to strengthen all democracies and free them from fascistic ideologies.

Title: "Contradiction in Action: Victor Esteban's Global Vs. Domestic Dichotomy"

Chapter 15: The Guardian of Left-wing Ideals and the Authoritarian Enigma

In the wake of criticism that he was supporting left-wing movements abroad as an "autocrat" who had stifled democracy at home, President Victor Esteban delivered a response aimed at rebuking his detractors and reasserting his global mission. His stance was unequivocal: he claimed his foray into global politics was not a campaign to export his authoritarian governance but a safeguard against the erosion of democracies by right-wing extremists.

"I won't let democracies fall," Esteban declared, positioning himself paradoxically as both a protector of democracy abroad and a consolidator of power within Attila. His robust defense was anchored in a commitment to perpetuating left-wing interest, which he equated with building a world resistant to what he labeled "fascistic ideologies." By this logic, Attila's autocracy was painted as an isolated necessity — a singular bastion of order and progressive values that merely served as an example of stability and growth amid a fractious global milieu.

Esteban artfully deflected the paradox of his stance by suggesting that Attila's one-party dominance provided a controlled experiment on governance, an example that while working domestically, did not represent a template for other nations. Instead, he insisted it was the ideological core of his policies — equality, social welfare, environmental stewardship — that he sought to promote, not the suppression of political competition.

His narrative framed Attila as an immune system booster for the world's democracies, an anchor point from which to strengthen their resistance against extremist currents. Esteban's rhetoric suggested that within the Attilan context, an 'enlightened' autocracy paradoxically served the grander aim of protecting and advancing democracies elsewhere.

The international reaction to Esteban's defense was mixed. Some on the international left, desperate for allies in the fight against ascendent populism, were willing to overlook the discrepancies in Esteban's domestic policies, emphasizing instead the aspirational elements of his international agenda. They echoed Esteban's sentiments that autocracy in Attila was unique and situational, and that the overall promotion of progressive values was a beneficial global service.

Others, including liberal democrats and right-wing opponents alike, dismissed Esteban's justification as an exercise in sophistry. They viewed Esteban's claim to be strengthening democracies as nothing more than a veil for expanding his ideology and influence, underscoring the contradiction between his restrictive rule in Attila and the democratic virtues he professed to foster abroad.

Critics pointed out that financial support to political parties based on ideological alignment risked the integrity and autonomy of the political landscapes within sovereign nations. The rhetoric around "freeing democracies from fascistic ideologies" was seen by some as a smokescreen for neo-imperialism, a twenty-first-century rendition of Cold War-era spheres of influence politicking.

By the mid-2020s, Victor Esteban had cemented his image both as an enigmatic figure and a polarizing force on the world stage. The chasm between his domestic autocracy and his professed dedication to global democracy left observers and historians grappling with a complex legacy: that of an unaging leader, a champion of the left, whose narrative wove through the contradictory threads of authoritarian rule at home and purported defense of democracy abroad. As an ever-present figure in the flux of international relations, Esteban remained at the heart of global debates over the true nature of freedom, governance, and the enduring question of whether the ends justify the means.
